examples.title: Recursive Tree Traversals
examples.description: Implementation of Preorder, Inorder, and Postorder traversals using recursion
examples.explanation: Recursive traversals use the call stack implicitly. Each function call handles one node and recursively processes subtrees in the specified order.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(h) where h is height of tree

examples.title: Iterative Inorder Traversal
examples.description: Inorder traversal without recursion using explicit stack
examples.code:
def inorder_iterative(root):
    stack = []
    current = root
    result = []
    
    while current or stack:
        # Go to leftmost node
        while current:
            stack.append(current)
            current = current.left
        
        # Current is None, pop from stack
        current = stack.pop()
        result.append(current.val)  # Visit node
        
        # Move to right subtree
        current = current.right
    
    return result
examples.explanation: Uses explicit stack to simulate recursion. First pushes all left children, then processes node and moves to right subtree.

examples.title: Level Order Traversal
examples.description: Breadth-First Search traversal of binary tree using queue
examples.explanation: Uses FIFO queue to process nodes level by level. Nodes are dequeued and their children are enqueued, ensuring level-order processing.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(w) where w is maximum width of tree

examples.title: Expression Tree Evaluation
examples.description: Building and evaluating expression tree using postorder traversal
examples.code:
class ExprNode:
    def __init__(self, val):
        self.val = val
        self.left = None
        self.right = None

def evaluate(node):
    if node is None:
        return 0
    
    # Leaf node (operand)
    if node.left is None and node.right is None:
        return int(node.val)
    
    # Evaluate left and right subtrees
    left_val = evaluate(node.left)
    right_val = evaluate(node.right)
    
    # Apply operator
    if node.val == '+':
        return left_val + right_val
    elif node.val == '-':
        return left_val - right_val
    elif node.val == '*':
        return left_val * right_val
    elif node.val == '/':
        return left_val / right_val

# Build tree for (3+4)*(5-2)
#         *
#        / \
#       +   -
#      / \ / \
#     3  4 5  2
root = ExprNode('*')
root.left = ExprNode('+')
root.left.left = ExprNode('3')
root.left.right = ExprNode('4')
root.right = ExprNode('-')
root.right.left = ExprNode('5')
root.right.right = ExprNode('2')

print(evaluate(root))  # Output: 21
examples.explanation: Expression trees are evaluated using postorder (left-right-root) which naturally gives operands before operator. This is equivalent to evaluating postfix notation.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of nodes in expression tree
